title: How to Deal with Spiritual Materialism
meta_description: Uncover the hidden trap of spiritual materialism. Learn to spot ego disguised as growth and find real transformation beyond crystals and credentials.
og_title: How to Deal with Spiritual Materialism
og_description: Uncover the hidden trap of spiritual materialism. Learn to spot ego disguised as growth and find real transformation beyond crystals and credentials.
body:
Spiritual Materialism Is a Trap - And You're Probably Already in It

You've been collecting crystals for years. You've got a bookshelf full of spiritual books you've barely cracked open. You've done the breathwork workshops, the shamanic journeys, the astrology deep-dives. You can talk about chakras, shadow work, and the law of attraction like a pro. But something feels... off. Hollow. Like you're building a beautiful spiritual resume while your actual life stays stuck in the same old patterns.

That's spiritual materialism. And it's a sneaky, seductive trap that almost everyone on this path falls into at some point. Maybe you're in it right now, and that's why you're here. Good. Let's talk about it.

Spiritual materialism is when you use spiritual practices and beliefs to prop up your ego instead of dissolving it. You're collecting experiences, identities, and spiritual "gear" because it makes you feel special, advanced, or superior. You're not actually changing - you're just adding spiritual stuff on top of the same old self.

Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche, the Tibetan Buddhist teacher who coined the term, put it bluntly: "We can deceive ourselves into thinking we are developing spiritually when instead we are strengthening our egocentricity through spiritual techniques."

The path is not about collecting spiritual trophies. It's about letting yourself be dismantled by truth, piece by piece, until there's nothing left to defend.

How to Know If You're in Spiritual Materialism (Be Honest)

You don't need to feel shame about this. We've all been there. But you do need to look at it straight. Here are the signs that your spirituality has become another ego project:

  • You're more invested in how you look spiritually than how you actually live. You post the altar photos, the meditation retreats, the "grateful" captions. But your real life - your relationships, your work, your inner world - hasn't shifted much.
  • You judge others who aren't as "awake" as you. You secretly feel superior to people who haven't done the work. You roll your eyes at coworkers who don't understand energy. You've got a spiritual pecking order in your head.
  • You're constantly chasing the next high. The next workshop, the next certification, the next plant medicine ceremony. You're addicted to spiritual experiences because they make you feel alive, but you can't sustain that feeling in your daily life.
  • You use spiritual language to avoid real feelings. "It's all good karma" when you're actually pissed. "They're just on their own path" when you're hurt. "I'm vibrating too high for this" when you're avoiding conflict.
  • You've made a spiritual identity that you defend. You're "the empath," "the lightworker," "the healer." And you're attached to that label. Someone challenges it? You get defensive.

Does any of that land? If it does, you're not broken. You're just doing what humans do - trying to find meaning and safety in a confusing world. But the path asks for something deeper than that.

Why You Fell for It (It's Not Your Fault)

Look, you didn't wake up one day and decide to be spiritually materialistic. It's a natural response to living in a culture that teaches you that more is always the answer. More stuff, more status, more experiences. You just applied that same logic to spirituality.

You've been told your whole life that something is wrong with you. That you're too sensitive, too emotional, too weird. So you found a spiritual approach that finally made you feel like you belonged. You found a language for your experiences. You found a community. And that felt amazing. So you started collecting more of it - more books, more practices, more identities - to cement that feeling of belonging and specialness.

But here's the thing: the real work isn't about building a better spiritual self. It's about seeing through the self altogether. The ego doesn't want to die. It wants to become the most enlightened ego in the room.

One of the most helpful books I've found on this exact topic is Cutting Through Spiritual Materialism by Chögyam Trungpa. It's the original source material and it's brutal in the best way. See on Amazon (paid link). If you're serious about untangling this, start there.

The Bright Wound - Why Your Sensitivity Is Both the Gift and the Trap

Here's where it gets personal. You're here because you feel things deeply. You always have. Your nervous system picks up on everything - other people's moods, the energy of a room, the unspoken tension in a conversation. You've been told you're too much. Too emotional. Too sensitive.

That sensitivity is your gift. It's what brought you to spirituality in the first place. You could feel that there was more to life than what the material world offered. You could sense the hidden layers. That's real. That's valid.

But here's the trap: that same sensitivity can become the raw material for spiritual materialism. You feel things deeply, so you start identifying as "the empath." You have intuitive hits, so you start building an identity around being "psychic." You've been wounded, so you turn your healing into a performance.

The Bright Wound is that place where your deepest sensitivity meets your deepest wounding. It's where you're most open and most defended at the same time. Spiritual materialism loves to live there.

You start using your spiritual gifts to avoid the actual wound. You "read the energy" instead of feeling your own feelings. You "transmute" instead of grieving. You "raise your vibration" instead of sitting with your rage.

The path forward is not to become more spiritual. It's to become more honest. To let your sensitivity serve your awakening, not your ego's need to feel special.

What to Do Instead - Practical Ways to Untangle from Spiritual Materialism

Okay, so you see it now. You can feel the ways you've been using spirituality to prop up your ego. What do you actually do about it? Here are some real, grounded practices that cut through the fluff.

1. Practice "Spiritual Minimalism"

Take a break from collecting. No new courses. No new books. No new practices. For a month, just work with what you already have. One meditation technique. One journaling practice. One way of connecting to the divine. Do it until it's boring. Do it until the novelty wears off. That's where the real work begins.

If you need a companion for this kind of deep, stripped-down practice, I recommend The Untethered Soul by Michael A. Singer. It's not about collecting anything. It's about letting go. See on Amazon (paid link).

2. Watch Your Language

Start noticing when you use spiritual language to avoid being human. Catch yourself saying "I'm vibrating too high for this" when you're actually scared. Notice when you say "they're on their own path" to avoid feeling hurt. Try replacing spiritual bypassing phrases with honest ones. "I'm angry." "I'm scared." "I don't know what to do." That's not less spiritual. That's more real.

3. Let Yourself Be a Beginner Again

Spiritual materialism loves expertise. It loves the feeling of knowing. So humble yourself. Try something you're bad at. Something that has nothing to do with your spiritual identity. Learn to garden. Take a pottery class. Play a sport. Let yourself suck at something. Let yourself be a beginner. The ego hates that. The soul loves it.

4. Question Your "Special" Identity

You're not "the empath." You're not "the lightworker." You're not "the healer." Those are roles your ego grabbed onto because they felt meaningful. But you are not your role. You are the awareness that holds all of it. Practice dropping the labels. When someone asks what you do, just say "I'm a person trying to be more awake." That's enough.

5. Bring Your Practice Off the Cushion

The real test of your spirituality is not how you feel during meditation. It's how you treat the person who cuts you off in traffic. It's how you handle being wrong. It's how you show up when no one is watching. If your spiritual practice doesn't make you more kind, more patient, more honest in your daily life, it's just another ego game. Right?!

The Hardest Truth - You Might Need to Give Up Your Spiritual Story

Here's the part no one wants to hear. You might need to let go of the story you've been telling yourself about your spiritual journey. The story where you're the hero, the one who woke up, the one who sees through the matrix. That story feels good. But it's also a cage.

Real awakening is not about having a better story. It's about having no story at all. It's about being so present that you don't need to define yourself as anything. You don't need to be "spiritual." You don't need to be "awake." You just need to be here, fully, without the constant commentary of the ego.

That's terrifying to the part of you that has built a whole identity around being spiritual. But it's also liberating. Because when you drop the spiritual identity, you get to just be yourself. Not the polished, enlightened version. The real version. The one who still gets annoyed, who still has doubts, who still doesn't know what the hell is going on.

That version is actually closer to the truth than any spiritual persona you could build.

How to Know You're Moving Through It

How do you know you're actually untangling from spiritual materialism, not just adding "letting go of spiritual materialism" to your spiritual resume? Here are some signs:

  • You care less about looking spiritual. You don't need to post about your practice. You don't need to tell people you meditated. You just do it, quietly, because it serves your life.
  • You're less judgmental of others. You can see someone who's deep in spiritual materialism and feel compassion instead of superiority. You remember being there yourself.
  • You can sit with not knowing. You don't need to have it all figured out. You don't need a spiritual approach for everything. Sometimes you just don't know, and that's okay.
  • Your practice is simpler. You've dropped the elaborate rituals. You don't need all the tools. You can sit in silence and just be present. That's enough.
  • You're more honest about your humanity. You admit when you're struggling. You don't spiritualize your pain. You just feel it and let it move through you.

Does that land? It's not glamorous, I know. There's no dramatic "ascension" moment. It's just a quiet, steady return to being real. And that's actually the whole point.

A Book That Will Help You See Through It All

I keep coming back to this one. Cutting Through Spiritual Materialism by Chögyam Trungpa is not an easy read. It will call you out. It will make you uncomfortable. But that's exactly what you need if you're serious about this path. Trungpa doesn't let you get away with anything. He sees through every spiritual game you're playing, and he names it with ruthless compassion. See on Amazon (paid link).

Another one that helped me is The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. I know, I know - it's the basic bitch of spiritual books. But there's a reason it's a classic. Tolle cuts through the spiritual ego with surgical precision. He doesn't care about your credentials. He just points you back to presence. See on Amazon (paid link).

The Bottom Line - You Don't Need to Be Special

Here's what I want you to take away from this. You don't need to be a spiritually advanced person. You don't need to be enlightened. You don't need to have all the answers. You just need to be honest. Honest about where you're still clinging. Honest about where you're using spirituality to avoid life. Honest about the parts of you that still want to be seen as special.

Your sensitivity is not a problem. It's a door. But it's a door that opens inward, not outward. It asks you to stop performing and start being. To stop collecting and start releasing. To stop climbing the spiritual ladder and realize you're already where you need to be.

The Bright Wound is not something to fix. It's something to hold. To be with. To let soften in the light of your own awareness. And that doesn't require any special technique. Just your full, honest presence.

You've got this. You've always had this. You just forgot because you got distracted by the shiny stuff. Come back. Sit down. Be still. That's the whole path.

If you want to go deeper into this and see where your own patterns of spiritual materialism are showing up, I've put together a simple assessment that can help you see it clearly. No fluff. No spiritual marketing. Just a mirror. Take the assessment here and see what's really driving your spiritual life.
